summary: This iteration represents a significant improvement over prior rounds (Iter 1: 5.5/10, Iter 2: 5/10 with fabricated claims). The paper has pivoted honestly to a negative-result framing, eliminated fabricated claims, and produced one genuinely supported positive finding (collision rate proxy, r=0.87, n=56). However, critical issues remain: (1) circular GT definition undermines the proxy validation, (2) only 7 GT pairs with huge variance makes UAD evaluation statistically fragile, (3) universal claims about token-level mutual exclusivity are extrapolated from a single cherry-picked example, (4) the F1 identity with random baseline is presented as a statistical finding when it is arithmetic, and (5) K-means achieving 85.7% recall is dismissed without analysis. The paper is honest and methodologically cleaner than predecessors, but the scientific contribution is still thin for a top-tier venue.

issues.description: Ground truth definition is circular: 'true absorption rate' (Section 3.2) and 'collision rate' (Section 3.4) are both defined as Jaccard overlap of top-K features. The Results present their correlation as 'proxy validation,' but it is self-correlation of the same metric. The pilot summary explicitly raised this concern: 'feature 11746 dominates all 26 letters, suggesting a first-letter super-feature rather than distributed absorption.' The paper never addresses whether the GT definition overcounts absorption.
issues.suggestion: Explicitly acknowledge the circularity. Distinguish between (a) the operational definition used for GT labeling and (b) the validation that collision rate recovers this operationalization. Add a paragraph discussing the feature 11746 dominance concern and why the correlation is still meaningful despite this limitation. Reframe 'proxy validation' as 'reliability of the operational definition.'

issues.description: Only 7 ground truth absorption pairs for UAD evaluation (one is a self-pair [24189, 24189], so 6 distinct pairs). With 6-7 positives and 4155 detected candidates, statistical power is extremely limited. The F1=0.00048 figure has huge variance---a single additional true positive would change F1 by ~40%. The paper presents this as definitive when it is based on a tiny sample. The writing review also flagged that one GT pair is a self-pair, affecting precision/recall denominators.
issues.suggestion: Add bootstrap confidence intervals for the F1 score. Acknowledge that the UAD evaluation is based on only 6-7 ground truth pairs and frame the conclusion as 'UAD fails on this test set' rather than 'UAD fails universally.' Clarify whether self-pairs are included in the GT count and recalculate metrics if needed.

issues.description: The paper claims 'absorption features are mutually exclusive at the token level' as a universal property, but this is only demonstrated for number words in a single constructed sequence. The evidence comes from one example ('one two three four five six seven eight'). Real-world hierarchical concepts (e.g., 'animal' and 'dog') DO co-occur in natural text ('the dog is an animal'). The universal claim in Discussion 5.1 ('It is a logical consequence of how language represents hierarchical concepts') is an overreach.
issues.suggestion: Soften the universal claim. State that mutual exclusivity holds for the tested hierarchies (numbers, punctuation) where child concepts are token-disjoint, but acknowledge that semantic hierarchies (animal/dog) may show different patterns. Add this as a stated limitation or test on at least one semantic hierarchy.

issues.description: The abstract claims 'F1 = 0.0005, indistinguishable from random sampling (same-cluster random F1 = 0.00048).' These values are identical to 4 significant figures, but this is a mathematical identity, not a statistical finding. Both UAD and same-cluster random have exactly 1 TP, 4154 FP, 6 FN. The F1 values MUST be identical by construction. Presenting this as an empirical comparison is misleading.
issues.suggestion: Reframe: 'UAD detects exactly 1 true positive out of 4155 candidates. Randomly sampling the same number of pairs from within clusters yields the same 1 true positive by chance. UAD's sophisticated pipeline provides zero improvement over this trivial baseline.' This is more honest and avoids presenting an identity as a statistical finding.

issues.description: The K-means ablation achieves 85.7% recall (6/7 TP) but the paper dismisses it as 'still failing.' This is inconsistent---85.7% recall is actually quite good for an unsupervised method. The issue is precision (0.043%), but the paper does not discuss whether the precision problem is addressable (e.g., by raising a similarity threshold post-clustering). Dismissing the best-performing variant without exploring why it succeeds where others fail is a missed analytical opportunity that could strengthen the constructive contribution.
issues.suggestion: Add analysis of why K-means succeeds where Ward linkage fails. Does K-means happen to group features by some non-co-occurrence property? Could a post-processing threshold on cluster coherence improve precision? This would strengthen the paper's constructive contribution and show intellectual curiosity.

issues.description: The novelty check was performed on the original positive-result UAD proposal, not the current negative-result paper. The novelty assessment states 'UAD as the first unsupervised absorption detection method' is novel, but the current paper is about why UAD FAILS. The novelty of 'UAD doesn't work' is different from 'UAD is a new method.' The core insight (co-occurrence clustering cannot detect mutually exclusive features) is logically trivial once stated---the value is in the empirical demonstration, not the insight itself.
issues.suggestion: Reframe novelty claims. The actual novel contributions are: (1) first systematic empirical evaluation of co-occurrence clustering for absorption detection, (2) identification of token-level mutual exclusivity as a structural barrier (for tested hierarchies), (3) validation of collision rate reliability at n=56 across hierarchy types. These are methodological observations, not new methods. Be honest that the contribution is incremental.

issues.description: The paper tests UAD on only one SAE (gpt2-small-res-jb, layer 8) with 1,000 sequences. The claim that 'the root cause is architecture-independent' (Proposal Section 2.2) is unsupported. Different SAE architectures (TopK, Gated, JumpReLU) may learn different feature structures. The conclusion that 'more SAEs would not change the conclusion' is an assertion, not evidence. The writing review also notes missing figures/tables and a fabricated claim about 'manual inspection of 50 false positives.'
issues.suggestion: Add at least one additional SAE test (different layer or architecture) or explicitly state this as a limitation. Remove the fabricated 'manual inspection of 50 false positives' claim. Generate the missing figures and tables referenced in the paper text. The claim about architecture-independence should be downgraded from assertion to hypothesis.

issues.description: The ablation results show that 'no dead filter' and 'no phi filter' produce IDENTICAL results to full UAD (same TP/FP/FN counts). This is suspicious---removing filtering steps should change the candidate set. The identical counts suggest these filtering steps are no-ops in this implementation (e.g., no features were actually filtered out). The paper does not explain this.
issues.suggestion: Investigate and report: how many features were actually removed by dead feature filtering? How many pairs were removed by phi filtering? If the filters removed nothing, explain why (e.g., all 500 selected features passed the variance threshold). This is important for interpreting the ablation results.

issues.description: The paper's tone oscillates between 'harsh rejection of co-occurrence' and 'constructive forward look.' In the Conclusion, it calls on the community to 'abandon co-occurrence-based approaches'---this is stronger than the evidence supports, since the paper only tested one type of co-occurrence clustering (UAD's specific pipeline) on one type of hierarchy (token-disjoint).
issues.suggestion: Soften the universal rejection language. Instead of 'abandon co-occurrence-based approaches,' say 'co-occurrence clustering as implemented in UAD is unsuitable for detecting token-disjoint hierarchical absorption.' Leave open the possibility that modified co-occurrence methods could work for semantic hierarchies where children co-occur.

issues.description: The F5 false positive analysis claims '99.98% false positive rate' but this is computed as FP/(TP+FP) = 4154/4155. This is the standard definition of 1 - precision, but the term 'false positive rate' in ML typically means FP/(FP+TN), which would be much lower. Using non-standard terminology without clarification could confuse readers.
issues.suggestion: Use standard terminology: report 'precision = 0.024%' rather than 'false positive rate = 99.98%.' If using the latter, explicitly define it as 1 - precision to avoid confusion with the standard FPR definition.

issues.description: Single seed (42) used throughout. While token-level mutual exclusivity is deterministic, the specific features identified (11513, 24189, etc.) and the clustering results may vary with different corpus samples or seeds. No sensitivity analysis is provided.
issues.suggestion: Add a brief sensitivity analysis: run UAD with 2-3 additional seeds and report variance in F1. If variance is low, this strengthens the conclusion. If high, it reveals fragility.

issues.description: The paper claims 'Our work establishes both a negative result that prevents the community from pursuing a dead-end direction' (Proposal Abstract). This overstates the impact. The paper tests one specific method (UAD) on one specific SAE with one specific type of hierarchy. It does not establish that the entire direction is a dead end.
issues.suggestion: Soften impact claims. 'Our work demonstrates that UAD's co-occurrence clustering approach fails for token-disjoint hierarchical absorption, suggesting the community should explore alternative approaches rather than tuning UAD hyperparameters.'

risks: Reviewer may dismiss the entire contribution as 'logically obvious'---once you state that co-occurrence clustering finds features that fire together while absorption features fire on different tokens, the empirical demonstration adds little.
The circular GT definition (collision rate = true absorption rate = same metric) may be flagged as a fundamental methodological flaw by any careful reviewer.
Only 6-7 GT pairs with a self-pair included makes the UAD evaluation statistically fragile; a reviewer may question whether any conclusion can be drawn from such a small sample.

The K-means result (85.7% recall) undermines the 'UAD is fundamentally flawed' narrative---if a simple clustering variant catches 6/7 pairs, the problem may be algorithm choice, not structural mismatch.
Single SAE, single layer, single model, single seed limits generalizability. A reviewer running the same experiment on a different SAE could get different results.
The paper targets NeurIPS/ICML but the contribution (one method fails on one SAE with 6 GT pairs) may be more appropriate for a workshop like ICBINB.
Missing figures and tables (referenced but not generated) is a red flag for submission readiness.
evidence_gaps: No independent validation of collision rate against Chanin et al.'s probe-based absorption definition---only self-correlation of the operational definition.
No test on semantic hierarchies (animal/dog) where children co-occur in natural text.
No multi-seed replication for any experiment.
No cross-SAE validation (different layer, architecture, or model).
No analysis of why K-means achieves 85.7% recall while Ward linkage achieves 14.3%.
No explanation for why dead feature and phi filtering are no-ops (identical results to full UAD).
No code repository link or pseudocode for UAD pipeline.
The 'manual inspection of 50 false positives' claim has no supporting evidence.
what_would_raise_score: To raise the score by 1 point (to 7.0): Fix the circular GT definition by explicitly reframing collision rate as reliability of operationalization (not proxy validation), add bootstrap CIs for F1, analyze why K-means succeeds, and soften universal claims to tested hierarchies. To raise by 2 points (to 8.0): Additionally, test on at least one semantic hierarchy (e.g., animal/dog), run multi-seed replication (3+ seeds), test on a second SAE layer or architecture, and generate all missing figures/tables. To reach 8.5+ (Strong Accept): Additionally, implement and pilot-test decoder weight similarity (proposed alternative) with actual results, expanding the contribution from 'this doesn't work' to 'here's what does.'
